coredata.dc:title: Azine based fluorescent rapid "off-on" chemosensor for detecting Th4+ and Fe3+ ions and its real-time application
coredata.dc:creator: Sawminathan, Sathish
Munusamy, Sathishkumar
Manickam, Saravanakumar
Jothi, Dhanapal
KulathuIyer, Sathiyanarayanan
coredata.dc:description: Biological toxicity and dangerous environmental pollution are inevitable when the level of Th4+ and Fe3+ exceeds the safety threshold. To address these problems, in this study, we report a dual-emission response chemosensor, HQCA, for rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of Th4+ and Fe3+ via colorimetric and fluorescence turn-on strategy. The absolute fluorescent "turn off" of HQCA, due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET), was immediately turned on with yellow fluorescence over the exposure to Th4+/Fe3+. The sensing capability of HQCA toward Th4+ and Fe3+ was studied in solution, and paper strips were fabricated with HQCA. Our results indicate excellent sensitivity and selectivity. Additionally, the value of emission intensity of HQCA had an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of Th4+/Fe3+, and the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be very low compared to reported methods and WHO norms. The interaction mechanism between HQCA and Th4+/Fe3+ was studied via Job's plot analysis, 1H NMR, Mass, and FT-IR analysis. Emission analysis of HQCA in the pH region 1–9 indicated that HQCA could successfully detect Th4+/Fe3+ in the pH region of 3–9. More importantly, the viability of HQCA to detect Th4+/Fe3+ ions were successfully tested in various water bodies and human serum albumin.

head.ce:title: Azine based fluorescent rapid "off-on" chemosensor for detecting Th4+ and Fe3+ ions and its real-time application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biological toxicity and dangerous environmental pollution are inevitable when the level of Th4+ and Fe3+ exceeds the safety threshold. To address these problems, in this study, we report a dual-emission response chemosensor, HQCA, for rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of Th4+ and Fe3+ via colorimetric and fluorescence turn-on strategy. The absolute fluorescent "turn off" of HQCA, due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET), was immediately turned on with yellow fluorescence over the exposure to Th4+/Fe3+. The sensing capability of HQCA toward Th4+ and Fe3+ was studied in solution, and paper strips were fabricated with HQCA. Our results indicate excellent sensitivity and selectivity. Additionally, the value of emission intensity of HQCA had an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of Th4+/Fe3+, and the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be very low compared to reported methods and WHO norms. The interaction mechanism between HQCA and Th4+/Fe3+ was studied via Job's plot analysis, 1H NMR, Mass, and FT-IR analysis. Emission analysis of HQCA in the pH region 1–9 indicated that HQCA could successfully detect Th4+/Fe3+ in the pH region of 3–9. More importantly, the viability of HQCA to detect Th4+/Fe3+ ions were successfully tested in various water bodies and human serum albumin.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, arduous research efforts have been dedicated to developing organic fluorescent materials, evident from the abundant publications in chemosensors, fluorescent biosensors, organic light-emitting diode materials (OLED), etc. [1–15]. With concerted effort in organic synthesis, complex chemical architectures can be designed, synthesized, and optimized in such a way that they can meet the practical needs of a fluorescent sensor. To this end, several organic fluorescent probes have been developed to detect biologically essential and toxic metal ions for bioimaging [16–30] and pollution inspection [31–45]. Thorium (Th4+) can be placed on the top of the list among the polluting toxic metal ions, as it is widely used in the nuclear industry. Th4+ is an abundantly available rare earth element that often co-exists with other rare earth elements in the form of monazite ((Ce, La, Nd, Th)PO4) [46,47]. Its crucial role in industrial products such as carbon arc lamps, welding materials, thorotrast imaging agents, alloys in jet engines, lantern mantles, and catalysts in the chemical industry cannot be ignored. However, its extensive use in various industrial products and copious thorium mining often leads to contamination of water, air, and soil bodies. Accumulation of Th4+, even in trace amounts, may induce severe pathological conditions, such as carcinogenesis, urinary system disease, and liver and lung damage [48,49]. This necessitates the achievement of the yet elusive goal of sensitive, onsite and selective detection of Th4+.

As an essential trace element in the human body, iron plays a pivotal role in various pathological and physiological processes, such as oxygen metabolism and electron transfer [50,51]. Cells maintain meticulous iron homeostasis for the survival of the living system since iron plays a pertinent role in transporting oxygen by being part of hemoglobin and serving in electron transport in the form of cytochromes. It has been found that aberrant Fe3+ fluctuation may initiate oxidative stress or ferroptosis, which is recently proclaimed as the potential cause of cell death [52,53]. In addition, severe human diseases, such as anemia, hemochromatosis, organ dysfunction, hepatitis, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease, are related to the ubiquitous presence of Fe3+ [54–56]. Therefore, developing a particular detecting modality for Fe3+ is vital for biomedical diagnosis.

Thus, detecting both Th4+ and Fe3+ is fundamental for pollution inspection and diagnosis of various disease states. Conventional approaches, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and voltammetry, suffer from complex sample preparation and tedious instrumentation procedure [57–59]. Furthermore, all these analytical techniques are subdued by the detection of only one analyte. Dual-mode detection methodology has proven to be more advantageous not only in efficiency but also on economic grounds. Fluorescent and colorimetric methods have captivated more attention due to their sensitivity and reliability. A few multitasking fluorescent probes that can detect two different transition metals have been reported in the past [60–63]. But a fluorescent probe that can report two cation species that belong to lanthanide and transition metal series would be advantageous as the circumstances involving one metal interfering with another can be avoided.

Notwithstanding this, a few single-mode fluorescent probes have been reported for Th4+ and Fe3+. Recently Wang et al. reported a new Th4+ fluorescent probe based on tetraphenyl-salicylaldehyde Schiff base in extreme acidic condition, albeit complete fluorescent enhancement found at pH = 3 [64]. Pyrene-antipyrine-based Schiff base has been used as the fluorescent turn-on sensor for Th4+ [65]. Fluorescent xanthene–aminoquinoline derivatives reported by Khurana et al. selectively detect Th4+ based on fluorescent quenching [66]. A fluorescent probe reported by Liu et al., based on β-diketone and 2-hydroxy-1-naphthaldehyde groups, has shown a selective turn-on response towards Th4+ [67]. Optically active 1,1′-bi-2-naphthol (BINOL) and its derivatives have been used as fluorescent turn-off-based sensors for Th4+ ions [68]. Rhodamine functionalized azo-phenol derivative has been reported for selective detection of Th4+ by the 'OFF-ON' mechanism [69]. Similarly, several fluorescent probes have been developed for Fe3+. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE)-active salicylaldehyde and 2-aminophenol based Schiff base detect Fe3+ by losing its AIE fluorescence [70]. Zhang et al. introduced pyrene-derivatized highly fluorescent carbon dots for the detection of Fe3+ [71]. Lim et al. have designed and synthesized indolizino[3,2-c] quinoline (IQ)-based AIE active fluorescent turn-on probe to detect Fe3+ [72]. Zhang et al. have used a triazole-based fluorescent turn-off probe for the detection of Fe3+ [73]. Recently developed optical probes for Fe3+ extend to metal−organic frameworks (MOFs) [74], bio composites [75], quantum dots [76–78], up conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [79] and carbon dots [80–83]. However, these probes are with limitations. They show poor selectivity, low sensitivity, tedious probe preparation method, single-mode detection, pH intolerance, fluorescent turn-off, and slow response to the analyte. This offers the possibility to develop the probes with an immediate turn-on response towards Th4+ and Fe3+.

Herein, we report facile and cost-effective synthesis of 9,9'-((1E,1′E)-hydrazine-1,2-diylidenebis(methaneylylidene))bis(benzo[h]quinolin-10-ol) (HQCA) (Scheme 1 ), which has fluorescent turn on and colorimetric response towards Th4+ and Fe3+. This probe has higher selectivity and sensitivity with low detection (LOD) than the reported methods. It has been found that this probe tolerates moderate acidity, and the detection of Th4+ is possible in pH as low as 3. Detection of Th4+ and Fe3+ has been successfully executed in real-time samples such as water and human serum albumin. The detection mechanism of HQCA towards Th4+ and Fe3+ was evaluated via NMR, ESI-MS, and IR spectroscopic methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and synthesis of probe HQCA
ce:section.ce:para: Probe HQCA was obtained by a simple, two-step synthesis with good yield, as shown in Scheme 1. First, the molecular characterizations of the probe were completely established by analytical techniques such as NMR and mass spectroscopic analysis (Fig. S2 and Fig.S6). Then, effective chemosensors were synthesized using a judicious coupling of a signaling unit (fluorophore) and a binding unit. Accordingly, we synthesized symmetrical azine, HQCA, with the reaction between 10-hydroxybenzo[h]quinoline-9-carbaldehyde and hydrazine (Scheme 1). This probe, as such, is not fluorescent due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) that occurs from the electronegative 'N' atom to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the excited fluorophore. However, the probe's fluorescence can be turned on upon the selective coordination with a metal ion, which blocks the PET process. Our study found that HQCA can selectively sense Th4+ and Fe3+ with color changes observable by the naked eye and employing UV–Visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible absorption spectral studies of HQCA for the detection of Th4+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–visible absorption spectrum of HQCA was recorded in acetonitrile/water (8:2) mixture solvent (Fig. S7). Intense spectral bands were observed at 279 nm (20750 mol−1dm3cm−1), 343 nm (14000 mol−1dm3cm−1), and 410 nm (21400 mol−1dm3cm−1) for the π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. To start with, colorimetric response of HQCA was evaluated with alkali metals, alkaline earth metals, transition metals and heavy metal ions (Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Bi2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Zn2+, La3+, Gd3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Pr3+, Ce4+ and Th4+). As shown in Fig. 1 a, none of the metal ions caused a significant absorption change in the ligand except Th4+ and Fe3+. When HQCA was exposed to Th4+, the absorption peak at 410 nm experienced a significant hypochromic shift, and a new peak appeared at 518 nm with the front shoulder peak at 485 nm. Further absorption at 343 nm was reduced significantly. Two isosbestic points were identified at 325 nm and 443 nm, which indicated a clear complex formation between Th4+ and HQCA. However, the addition of Fe3+ to a solution of HQCA led to similar changes in the absorption at 410 nm, 518 nm, and 485 nm. But the peak at 343 nm got completely disappeared, and a new peak appeared at 360 nm, which led to different isosbestic points of 380 nm and 440 nm. This implies that coordination between Fe3+ and HQCA is slightly different from that of Th
4+ and HQCA. The concentration of Th4+ required was slightly more than that of Fe3+ required to attain the plateau in absorption changes in HQCA. This can be due to the different complexation abilities and kinetics of Th4+ and Fe3+ towards HQCA. In addition, HQCA exhibited distinct visible color change from greenish-yellow to dark yellow over the exposure to Th4+ and Fe3+, as shown inFig. 1b.

Furthermore, incremental addition of Th4+ and Fe3+ brought about linear changes in the absorption of HQCA with an R2 value of 0.9987 and 0.9941, as shown in Fig. 1c and d. The binding constant ( K ) of HQCA with Th4+ was calculated to be 1.72 × 104 M−1, using Benesi–Hildebrand (B–H plot) linear curve fitting expression by plotting 1/ΔA vs. 1/[Th4+] (Fig. 2 a). Similarly, K for HQCA + Fe
3+ was calculated to be 2.44 × 103 M−1 using the B–H plot (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recognition of Th4+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
In acetonitrile, the free ligand (2 × 10−5 M) exhibited weak emission when excited at 442 nm due to the PET process between electronegative 'N' atom and hydroxyquinoline (Fig. S8). The quantum yield of the native probe was calculated to be Φ = 0.001. Upon adding Th4+, HQCA showed a dramatic increase in the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 532 nm with a Stokes shift value of 3,828 cm−1. It was found that HQCA concentration of 2 × 10−5 M showed a 486-fold increase in the intensity over the addition of 5.5 equivalents Th4+. The fluorescence quantum yield of the solution increased from 0.009 to 0.3. Whereas slightly red-shifted emission (λem = 548 nm) compared to Th
4+
-HQCA was observed when HQCA was treated with Fe3+ (Fig. S9) with Stokes shift value was calculated to be 4,376 cm−1. Solution of HQCA (2 × 10−5 M) recorded a 382-fold increase in the emission intensity over the addition of 4.6 equivalents Fe3+. For HQCA + Fe
3+ fluorescence, the quantum yield was calculated to be 0.32. It is important to note that the emission property of an organic ligand containing nitrogen and oxygen is highly susceptible to the water present in the working medium. Thus, the emission property of free HQCA and HQCA + Th
4+
/Fe
3+ was meticulously analyzed by gradually increasing the water fraction in an acetonitrile medium (Fig. S10). It was found that the emission spectra of free HQCA do not undergo any noticeable change. In contrast, the turned-on emission of HQCA + Th
4+ or HQCA + Fe
3+ undergoes very little fluorescence change up to the water fraction of 20%. Further increase in the water fraction led to significant quenching of HQCA + Th
4+ /Fe
3+ emission spectra. Hence, we continued our analysis in the acetonitrile/water (8:2) mixture. As with the absorption experiment of HQCA with various metal ions, the emission response of HQCA with different metal ions was also carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 a, except for Th4+ and Fe3+, none of the metal ions brought about a significant change in the fluorescence property of HQCA. In addition, bright yellow emission was observed from a completely colorless solution when irradiated under a UV lamp (Fig. 3c).

LOD values were calculated to be 2.1 nM and 3.3 nM for Th4+ and Fe3+ (Fig. S11 and Fig. S12), respectively. To substantiate the obtained LOD value, we added the calculated LOD concentration to the solution of HQCA and recorded its emission spectra. As shown in Fig. S8, there is a conspicuous increase in the fluorescence spectra of the ligand, indicating the reliability of HQCA in the detection of Th4+/Fe3+. The found LOD value is much lower than the maximum residue limit (Th4+ = 2.7 × 10−7 M, Fe3+ = 5.4 × 10−6 M) recommended by WHO (0.27 μM for Th4+ [65] and 5.36 μM for Fe3+ [84]). In addition, LOD obtained by our probe is lower than those found in other recently reported methods (Table S1 and S2), indicating that HQCA can be efficiently applied in various platforms for the real-time detection of Th4+ and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference of other metal ions and pH
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing ability of the HQCA towards Th4+ and Fe3+ was tested in the presence of other interfering metal ions. It was done on the assumption that other metal ions could quench the turned-on emission over the presence of Th4+ or Fe3+. We observed that the emission intensity value was not disturbed by other interfering metal ions (Fig. S9, Fig. S13 and Fig. S14). This suggests the non-interference of different metal ions and the excellent sensing selectivity of HQCA. Detection of the metal ions in strong to moderate acidic conditions is also essential for the versatile utilization of the sensor, as the metal extraction and isolation involve strong acidic conditions. Unlike N-lone pairs in imine functionality, we envisioned that azines would be less perturbed by H+ ions as N-lone pairs are weak nucleophiles. Hence, to investigate the sensitivity of HQCA towards H+ ions, pH titrations were carried out. As shown in Fig. 5 a, HQCA had insignificant emissions when exposed to a pH range of 9 to 3, but the emission intensity increased sharply when pH decreased below 3. The emission intensity reached the maximum when the pH was maintained at 1. This could be due to the decomposition of HQCA, which may exude 10-hydroxybenzo[h]quinoline-9-carbaldehyde, which is emissive by nature. As expected, a low pH solution (pH = 1 and 2) of HQCA does not bring any emission changes over the addition of Th4+ and Fe3+ since HQCA may not be available in its native form to interact with metal ions. While the pH value was at 3, emission intensity reached a 75% increment. At pH range from 4 to 9, the fluorescence of the probes reached its maximum over the addition of Th4+ and Fe3+ (Fig. 5b). From the above results, we can infer that metal ions influence the fluorescence emission in the pH ranges of 4–9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stoichiometry of binding and mechanism for sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The Job's plot experiment of HQCA with Th4+ and Fe3+ complexation was carried out, as shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b. The results indicated that for HQCA + Fe
3+ and HQCA + Th
4+
, the inflection point appeared at fHQCA = 1:1. Furthermore, the results indicated a similar binding stoichiometry between HQCA + Fe
3+
and HQCA + Th
4+
. These results were further substantiated by recording mass spectra for the HQCA solution by adding excess Th4+ and Fe3+ (Fig. 6c and d). The peak at m/z = 672.2 corresponding to [HQCA + Th
4+
] and m/z = 495.8 corresponding to [HQCA + Fe
3+ ] coincides precisely with the results obtained from Job's plot experiment. The sensing mechanism of HQCA was further investigated by further extending the study to 1H NMR and FT-IR experiments. The 1H NMR spectral data of HQCA before and after the addition of Th4+/Fe3+ are depicted in Fig. S15 and Fig. S16. In [HQCA + Fe
3+ ], all protons of HQCA slightly shifted to the up-field region. The imine protons and the hydroxyl proton got well shifted after the complexation, whereas the hydroxyl proton in [HQCA + Th
4+ ] had a slight downfield shift, and all the other protons have shown up field shift. The structure and the binding mechanism were further evaluated by FT-IR analysis of free HQCA and the complex (Fig. 6e). HQCA revealed clear and strong characteristic absorption bands for -OH (3583 cm−1), CN (1710 cm−1), and C–O (1220 cm−1) stretching in IR spectra. These absorption bands underwent a significant shift to 3477, 1707, and 1224 cm−1, respectively, in the case of HQCA + Th
4+ . IR spectra of HQCA + Fe3+,-OH, and -C-O stretching bands shifted to 3570 and 1222 cm-1 while the -CN absorption band was not affected. This indicates that Th4+/Fe3+ complexes with HQCA via -HO and CN functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to paper-based system for the detection of Th4+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: It is crucial to develop sensitive, rapid, and onsite detection approaches to inspect potential environmental pollution. Accordingly, we have developed a portable and practical HQCA paper-based sensor by fabricating HQCA on the test paper to investigate its potential as fast field metal sensing. The well-known drop-casting method was used to disperse HQCA to the standard lab filter paper homogeneously. Its visual detection was investigated by exposing various concentrations of metal ion solution. Fluorescence changes of the test paper were monitored under the 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 7 a, yellow color fluorescence gradually appeared with the increasing concentration of the metal (Th4+/Fe3+). The selectivity of the HQCA paper-based sensor for the detection of (Th4+/Fe3+) was thoroughly evaluated by treating the test paper with various interfering metal ions as studied in UV–visible and fluorescence experiments. As presented in Fig. 7b, HQCA paper-based sensor changed color to yellow fluorescence only in the presence of Th4+/Fe3+, while other metal ions did not influence color change. To sum up, the development of the HQCA paper-based sensor could successfully achieve the sensitive, rapid, precise, and on-site visual detection of Th4+/Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Th4+& Fe3+ ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical utilization of HQCA to determine Th4+ & Fe3+was validated by the applicability in real-time water samples, as listed in Table 1 . Water samples collected from various water resources had been spiked with the known concentration of Th4+ & Fe3+ ions. The metal content in the prepared samples was analyzed by recording fluorescence spectra. As shown in Table 1, the presented sensor's quantitative measurement was in agreement with the known spiked Th4+/Fe3+ content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of HQCA was further extended to detect Fe3+ levels in human serum samples. As shown in Fig. S17, with the incremental addition of deproteinized human serum sample, the fluorescence intensity of HQCA gradually increased, and the fluorescence showed a linear relation with the volume of the human serum sample (Fig. S18). For the quantitative estimation of Fe3+ in the human serum sample, a standard linear curve was generated by adding a known concentration of FeCl3 to HQCA, as shown in Fig. 8 a. Fluorescence intensity obtained for 0.5 μL human serum sample was extrapolated to the x-axis, and the concentration of Fe3+ in a selected human serum was calculated to be 0.9 ± 0.05 μM, which almost matched with the value (1 μM) obtained by ICP-MS (Fig. 8b).The results indicated that the HQCA-based fluorescence probe was capable of detecting Fe3+ in the human serum sample. These results demonstrate the high analytical precision and feasibility of HQCA towards the detection of Fe3+ in environmental and biological samples.

In summary, we successfully designed and synthesized a new, elegant and economic azine-based fluorescence sensor (HQCA). This probe exhibited high selective and sensitive fluorescence "turn on" response towards Th4+ and Fe3+with different emission wavelengths. The Job's and Benesi- Hildebrand plots demonstrated 1:1 binding stoichiometry between HQCA and Th4+/Fe3+, which was in good agreement with mass spectral analysis. The calculated fluorometric detection limit is in the nano molar range, substantially lower than those found in many recent reports. The recognition of Th4+and Fe3+ by probe HQCA has insignificant interference from other potential competitive cations. The binding mechanism between HQCA and Th4+/Fe3+ was studied using 1H NMR, FTIR, and ESI-mass spectroscopy techniques. The ligand, HQCA, was successfully implemented to detect Th4+/Fe3+ for water samples collected from various water bodies. Real-time application of HQCA was further extended to detect Fe3+ in human serum samples, and the result coincided well with ICP-MS analysis. As a potential sensing candidate, a handy laboratory test paper with HQCA was fabricated for the selective and sensitive detection of Th4+/Fe3+.

Copies of 1H/13CNMR and HRMS/LCMS spectra of compound HQCA and HQCA + Fe
3+ and HQCA + Th
4+ complex.

All the chemicals were purchased from commercial sources, and no further purification was carried out. Reactions were monitored using TLC (thin-layer chromatography), and the compounds were purified using silica gel. Standard solutions of ligand (2 × 10−5M) were prepared in AR-grade acetonitrile solvent. Nitrates, sulfates and chlorides of different metal ions were dissolved in double distilled water to prepare various metal ion solutions (Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Bi2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Zn2+, La3+, Gd3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Pr3+, Ce4+, Fe3+ and Th4+) with stock concentration of 1 × 10−3 M.

UV–visible spectra were obtained on JASCO V-730 UV–visible spectrophotometer using a 1 cm quartz cell. Emission spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. Solution infrared spectra were recorded using Shimadzu affinity Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer in the range of 4000−400 cm−1. ADVANCE Bruker (400 MHz) NMR spectrometer recorded NMR spectra with tetramethylsilane as an internal standard. Mass spectra were obtained on an Agilent 6495 LCMS-MS instrument.

Fluorescence and absorption measurements were performed as follows: Appropriate volume from a stock solution of metal ions (1 × 10−3 M, H2O) was added to the 2 mL of HQCA (2 × 10−5 M, CH3CN) solution. The absorption and fluorescence of the solution were run immediately after the addition of metal ions. The excitation and emission slit width of fluorescence measurements was 5 nm, and the excitation wavelength was 442 nm. To determine Fe3+ in the human serum sample, deproteinized human serum sample was prepared according to the literature procedure. Aliquots of the above deproteinized human serum samples were added to 2 mL of HQCA solution, and the emission spectra were recorded immediately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic procedure and characterization of HQCA

ce:section.ce:para:
Anhydrous hexamethylenetetramine (0.55 g, 3.9 mmol) and 10-hydroxybenzo[h]quinoline (0.51 g, 2.6 mmol) were dissolved in glacial acetic acid (AcOH) (10 mL). The reaction mixture was refluxed at 120 °C and stirred for 4 h. Then, the reaction was cooled to room temperature. After adding water to the reaction mixture, the organic layer was extracted with chloroform. The organic phase was collected and dried over Na2SO4 and filtered. The solvent was evaporated in vacuo, and the resulting mixture was purified by silica gel column chromatography using ethyl acetate: hexane (1:9) as eluent. Yellow solid of compound 1 was obtained in a 40% yield. Melting point - 206-210 °C. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) 16.09 (s, 1H, OH), 10.80 (s, 1H, CHO), 8.88 (s, ArCH), 8.35 (d, J = 8 Hz, 1H), 8.09 (d, J = 8 Hz, 1H), 7.80 (s, 2H), 7.65 (t, J = 8 Hz, 1H), 7.39 (d, J = 8 Hz, 1H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3):
δ
189.70 (ArCO), 164.69 (ArC), 148.31 (ArC), 145.69 (ArCH), 139.05 (ArCH), 137.04 (ArC), 128.92 (ArCH), 127.78 (ArC), 127.17 (ArCH), 126.43 (ArCH), 121.49 (ArC), 120.67 (ArCH), 118.12 (ArCH),116.30 (ArC).

Compound 1 (89 mg, 0.4 mmol) and hydrazine hydrate solution (10 μL, 0.2 mmol) were dissolved in ethanol (8 mL). The reaction mixture was reflexed for 1hr. Then, the reaction was cooled at room temperature. Yellowish precipitate was collected by filtration, washed with ethanol and further dried at room temperature. Yellow solid of compound HQCA was obtained in 45% yield. Melting point greater than 300 °C.1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3)
δ
16.09 (s, 2H, OH), 9.36 (s, 2H), 9.09 (s, 2H), 8.71 (d, J = 4 Hz, 2H), 8.42 (d, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 8.06 (s, 4H), 7.90 (t, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 7.66 (d, J = 8 Hz, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3)
δ
:164.42 (2 × ArC), 161.97 (2 × ArCH), 148.73 (2 × ArCH), 128.89 (2 × ArC), 128.80 (2 × ArC), 128.09 (2 × ArCH), 127.60 (2 × ArCH), 127.52 (2 × ArCH), 127.49 (2 × ArC), 125.74 (2 × ArC), 124.39 (2 × ArCH), 122.38 (2 × ArCH), 116.57 (2 × ArC), 116.35 (2 × ArC). HRMS (ESI-MS) for C28H18N4O2 Calculated [M+1] m/z 442.1430, Found: 443.2153.

Sathish Sawminathan: was doing all experimental work. Sathishkumar Munusamy: Data curation, prepared the manuscript using the data and scientific interpretation. Saravanakumar Manickam: was doing all experimental work. Dhanapal Jothi: was doing all experimental work. Sathiyanarayanan KulathuIyer: Data curation, prepared the manuscript using the data and scientific interpretation.

SM gratefully acknowledges the youth 1000 Talent Program of China and the Interdisciplinary Research Program of Hunan University. The DST-FIST NMR facility at VIT India is duly acknowledged. The authors thank Dr.R. Srinivasan, SLL-VIT, for language editing.
